channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: Donald Trump’s blind alley
item.description: America’s president looks bereft of good options for solving the stand-off in the Gulf

item.title: Russia Shifts Its Focus to the Black Sea
item.description: Moscow is targeting trade routes and deepwater ports to strain Ukraine’s wartime economy.

item.title: Will Hasina Return to Bangladesh?
item.description: The former prime minister set a timeline for her homecoming, despite the threat of arrest.

item.title: The rate at which Earth is absorbing energy is alarming climate scientists
item.description: Reflections on a warming planet

item.title: Earth is dimming
item.description: The planet is reflecting less light back into space. That is making the world hotter

item.title: How to make AI safe—and free of America and China
item.description: State-backed efforts to catch up with frontier models are doomed

item.title: A New Law Heralds China’s Fraught Ethnic Future
item.description: Xi Jinping’s push for assimilation won’t solve long-standing issues.

item.title: Sudan Faces New Sanctions as Evidence of Atrocities Mounts
item.description: But countries remain unwilling to directly sanction foreign actors enabling the war.

item.title: Ukraine Isn’t Waiting Around for Patriots
item.description: A new European coalition is looking to build quicker and cheaper anti-ballistic missile systems—off Ukrainian technology.

item.title: Eli Lilly is reinventing the pharma business
item.description: The world’s largest drugmaker is betting big on preventive medicines—and learning from big tech

item.title: Ukraine’s Drones Have Turned the Tide, but Can They Change the Map?
item.description: Mid-range drone strikes may not be enough to regain Ukrainian territory.

item.title: China’s trade gap is narrowing. And other surprises
item.description: The world’s second-biggest economy is stumbling into fiscal austerity

item.title: Meet the committee to buy Europe
item.description: The continent’s dealmakers deserve much more attention

item.title: How to preserve Africa’s architectural gems
item.description: Development and restoration must go hand in hand

item.title: The Iran War Exposes the Global Economy’s Fault Lines
item.description: Buoyed by AI stocks, global financial markets have largely shrugged off the war with Iran. Yet the conflict’s long-term economic costs are only beginning to emerge, as depleted strategic oil reserves, damaged refining capacity, and rising fuel and fertilizer costs increasingly weigh on lower-income economies.

item.title: Xi Jinping expels another leader from his top team
item.description: He has set a record for booting out members of the Politburo

item.title: Governments Should Govern AI, Not Own It
item.description: AI systems are now powerful enough to threaten critical infrastructure, financial systems, and national security. Rather than becoming shareholders in AI companies, as US President Donald Trump has proposed, governments should invest in the independent institutions needed to hold the industry accountable.

item.title: The Other American Anniversary
item.description: The 1826 Congress of Panama sought to resist the predations of great powers. It was as relevant then as it is now.

item.title: Australia Wants to ‘Manage’ AI. What Will That Look Like?
item.description: “This is our time to decide what AI looks like here in Australia,” Prime Minister Albanese said.

item.title: India’s Monsoon Deluge Reflects Intensifying Global Climate Emergency
item.description: From floods to heatwaves, cascading climate disasters expose mounting vulnerabilities demanding coordinated scientific and policy responses.

item.title: The China Factor in Cuba-US Tensions
item.description: Insights from Orlando J. Pérez

item.title: Trump’s Boat Strikes Are Crimes Against Humanity
item.description: Rodrigo Duterte’s prosecution at the International Criminal Court shows why U.S. officials should be worried.

item.title: The Tragedy of “Westalgia”
item.description: Some European leaders seem to think that abasing themselves for Donald Trump's pleasure is necessary to save NATO and keep the Western alliance alive. But by so desperately clinging to the good old days of the transatlantic relationship, Europe’s leaders are risking more than just their dignity.

item.title: South Korea’s Biggest Criminal Justice Reform
item.description: Controversy is raging over how – and how much – to shave the prosecutors’ inordinate powers.

item.title: Amid the Afghanistan-Pakistan Conflict, India-Taliban Diplomatic Ties Are Strengthening
item.description: India’s diplomatic outreach to the Taliban has increased tremendously over the last year.

item.title: China Can’t Talk About Birth Rates Without Talking About Rural Pensions
item.description: The need to support elderly parents, particularly in rural areas, is an underappreciated factor in China's declining birth rate.

item.title: Putin’s War Comes Home to Russia
item.description: Ukraine’s attacks have unleashed a crisis—but don’t expect repercussions for the regime.

item.title: Argentina v England is one of the most intense rivalries in sport
item.description: The two countries have not played each other in over two decades

item.title: How the United States and Iran Can Solve Hormuz
item.description: Fees for passage through the strait are likely inevitable.

item.title: The Film ‘Satluj’ Revisits a Turbulent Chapter in India’s Counterinsurgency History
item.description: A biopic on human rights activist Jaswant Singh Khalra puts the spotlight on thousands of enforced disappearances and secret cremations carried out by the Punjab Police.

item.title: The West’s Turkey Dilemma
item.description: Few countries better illustrate the difficult trade-off between democratic principles and strategic necessity than Turkey. As the country’s geopolitical and economic importance grows, NATO allies are becoming increasingly willing to overlook President Recep Tayyip Erdoğan’s attacks on democratic institutions.

item.title: How the Tech Lords Hacked the Firm
item.description: A handful of tech oligarchs have figured out how to tap public markets without submitting to shareholder oversight. The recent SpaceX IPO represents the culmination of this process, granting Elon Musk near-total authority and revealing how thoroughly the mechanisms meant to hold corporate leaders accountable have been weakened.

item.title: Pakistan’s Operation Shaban Targets Both the TTP and BLA in Balochistan
item.description: Operation Shaban is in response to a deadly terrorist attack on July 7 in the Mangi Dam area, which claimed the lives of 27 police personnel.

item.title: What Did the Nine-Dash Line Ruling Accomplish?
item.description: Ten years on, the Philippines is stepping up its defense plans in the South China Sea.

item.title: NATO Is Splitting in Two
item.description: Mark Carney and Mark Rutte offer competing visions for the future of the trans-Atlantic alliance.

item.title: The World Is Giving Up on America
item.description: This time anti-Americanism is different.

item.title: China Is Sabotaging the World That Enables Its Rise
item.description: Beijing’s risky bet against the West.

item.title: Southeast Asia Has Weathered the Hormuz Crisis
item.description: The region has shown real evidence of resilience.

item.title: Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan Grit Their Teeth Amid Growing Central Asian Fuel Crisis
item.description: With Ukrainian drone attacks putting the pinch on Russia’s oil industry, the Central Asian region faces a fuel crisis too.

item.title: New Caledonia Has a New, Pro-France President. What Now?
item.description: New Caledonia has a new government, but the Pacific territory remains deeply divided between pro-independence and loyalist factions.
